cases.description: Default basis 0 (US/NASD 30/360): treats every month as 30 days. Days = 6*30+29 = 209, over a 360-day year -> 209/360. Source: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/yearfrac-function-3844141e-c76d-4143-82b6-208454ddc6a8 (worked example rounds to 0.58055556)

basis 1 (Actual/actual): actual 211 calendar days between the two dates, divided by 366 since the enclosing 1-year span (Jan 2012-Jan 2013) is a leap year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs worked example rounds to 0.57650273.
cases.expected_note: 211/366 = 0.5765027322404372, matching Microsoft's documented rounded example (0.57650273) to 8 decimal places. Actual/actual leap-year-boundary handling is a documented high-divergence area across spreadsheet engines.

cases.description: basis 2 (Actual/360): actual 211 calendar days divided by a fixed 360-day year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs basis table.
basis 3 (Actual/365): actual 211 calendar days divided by a fixed 365-day year. Source: Microsoft YEARFRAC docs worked example rounds to 0.57808219.
cases.expected_note: 211/365 = 0.5780821917808219, matches Microsoft's documented rounded example exactly.

cases.description: basis 4 (European 30/360). For this particular date pair neither day-of-month is the 31st, so the European day-count adjustment never triggers and the result equals basis 0's 209/360.
cases.expected_note: European 30/360 only differs from US 30/360 when a start/end day-of-month is 31; with day 1 and day 30 as here, both conventions compute 209/360 identically.

cases.description: basis values outside 0-4 are documented as invalid.
cases.expected_note: Microsoft docs: 'invalid basis values (< 0 or > 4) return a #NUM! error.'
